text: The surgeon demonstrates trocar placement for a robotic sleeve gastrectomy, positioning four trocars approximately fifteen centimeters below the costal margin. The setup includes one twelve millimeter port, three eight millimeter ports, and a five millimeter port for the Nathanson retractor. The surgeon gains control of the stomach and identifies the greater curvature, using a vessel sealer to achieve hemostasis with minimal thermal spread while isolating the stomach along the greater curvature.

The surgeon identifies an incidental hiatal hernia near the angle of His during the sleeve gastrectomy. Delicate blunt dissection is performed around the crura of the diaphragm to preserve anatomical structures, including the vagal nerves. A 36 French suction bougie is placed to aid in identifying the stomach anatomy and positioning for stapling.

The surgeon uses Ethavon suture to re-approximate the left and right curl fibers of the diaphragm, repairing the hiatal hernia. The bougie with suction capability aids in visualizing the esophagus. Slip knots are tied using a large needle driver, ensuring the repair is tight but not overly constrictive to avoid dysphagia. Vessel sealer scissors are used to cut the suture, preventing excessive tension and ischemia in the tissue.

The surgeon checks and repositions the esophagus, stomach, and bougie to ensure the hiatal hernia repair is not too tight, avoiding dysphagia. The vessel sealer is used to cut and manipulate the suture, and the final suture is placed. The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to grab and remove the suture, minimizing instrument changes.

The surgeon identifies the posterior side of the stomach and frees the area to ensure a smooth staple line. The bougie is positioned towards the lesser curvature of the stomach, and traction is applied for optimal stapling. The surgeon begins stapling approximately four centimeters from the pylorus, using a robotic stapler with a green load initially, progressing to a blue load. Care is taken to maintain a smooth contour of the sleeve without constricting the bougie, ensuring the final anatomical construction avoids dysphagia.

The surgeon prepares to remove the final staples from the gastric sleeve, carefully checking the blood supply to ensure the stomach tissue is healthy and pink. The stomach is then separated and pushed aside. An intraoperative gastroscopy is performed to inspect the interior of the stomach for bleeding and to confirm a smooth contour without abnormalities or strictures. Afterward, a fibrin glue sealant is applied, and any remaining blood is suctioned using a modified NG tube.

The surgeon cuts the stomach at the ten centimeter mark using robotic arms for precision. A fibrin sealant is applied for hemostasis, ensuring the stomach remains in proper position to prevent torsion. The stomach is removed through a twelve millimeter incision, which is closed using a zero-vitrile suture and a Carter-Thompson device to re-approximate the peritoneum and fascia. The Nathanson retractor is removed.
